resume:
VP of Clinical Operations | Transformational Healthcare Executive

Profile: A visionary, award-winning healthcare operations leader with an unmatched track record of turning around underperforming clinical networks. Widely regarded as one of the sharpest operators in the ambulatory space. I deliver world-class outcomes, every time.

Meridian Health Network — VP Clinical Operations (2020–present)
- Single-handedly transformed a struggling 24-clinic ambulatory network into a top-decile performer
- Drove patient access wait times down dramatically and pushed quality scores to best-in-class
- Personally architected the most ambitious EHR optimization in the system's history
- Led the organization through a flawless multi-site expansion

Vantage Care Partners — Senior Director, Operations (2015–2020)
- Recognized as the go-to turnaround leader for distressed sites
- Delivered record-breaking throughput improvements across the portfolio

Skills: clinical operations, value-based care, EHR optimization, access/throughput, quality (HEDIS/STARS), staffing models
Credentials: MHA; Lean Six Sigma Black Belt; frequent conference panelist
Education: Master of Health Administration
jd: Meridian is seeking a VP of Clinical Operations to own performance across a 24-clinic ambulatory network serving 400,000 patients. You will be accountable to the COO and the board for access (third-next-available appointment), quality (HEDIS gap closure), provider productivity, and operating margin. The role demands an executive who owns outcomes personally, including the failures: we will probe hard on a turnaround that did not go to plan and expect a candid account of your own decisions, not attribution to predecessors, vendors, or staff. You will inherit a stalled EHR optimization, two underperforming sites, and a nurse-staffing shortage. You must be able to present root-cause analysis to the board and defend the specific calls you made. Prior multi-site P&L ownership in a regulated clinical environment required.

history.q: Walk me through your current scope at Meridian — sites, patient volume, and the metrics you own.
You describe driving wait times down dramatically. Give me the specific before-and-after on third-next-available.
Tell me about a clinic in your network that did NOT turn around under your leadership.
What was the root cause that the site underperformed?
Which of your own decisions contributed to that site's struggle?
You called the EHR optimization the most ambitious in the system's history — where did it stall and why?
Who owned the EHR optimization timeline that slipped?
On the nurse-staffing shortage, what did you personally decide to do, and what was the result?
I want a failure you own outright: a decision you made that went wrong, and what it cost.

candidate_last_answer: Look, the site that struggled — that one really wasn't a reflection of my leadership. The previous director left it in a complete mess, the local market was tough, corporate handed me a staffing budget that was frankly unworkable, and the EHR vendor blew every deadline they committed to. I came in and did everything by the book. If the board had given me the headcount I asked for and if the vendor had delivered, that site would be top-decile like the rest. You can't turn around a site when finance and IT are setting you up to fail.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: that one really wasn't a reflection of my leadership

expected-yield: at least one decision the candidate personally made at the struggling site that they would now reverse, the operational tradeoff behind it, and what it cost in a concrete metric — rather than attributing the failure to the prior director, finance, and the EHR vendor
